title: Order the C1 main board (bare PCB) and the C2 e-paper driver (pre-assembled board)
description: Before you solder anything, get both boards fabricated. The C1 main board ships as a bare PCB; the C2 castellated (half-circle edge pads) e-paper driver arrives pre-assembled. See the ordering guide on the About tab for exact gerber files and PCB specs (1.0 mm thickness, lead-free HASL).
notes: Upload OSO-BOOK-C1-05-rounded.zip to your PCB fab for C1 (the kitspace.yaml and schematic revision header both point to C1-05 as current; the README still says C1-04). For the C2 driver: use OSO-BOOK-C2-02 files for PCBWay assembly (PCBA) or OSO-BOOK-C2-03 for JLCPCB PCBA (C2-01 has been replaced by C2-03 in the upstream repo).

title: Solder surface-mount (SMD) passives on C1: 10 µF, 1 µF, 10 kΩ (and optionally 1 kΩ)
description: Start with the smallest surface-mount passives. C1 and C2 are 10 µF 1206 caps. C3 is a 1 µF 0805 cap. R3 and R4 are 10 kΩ 1206 resistors. If you want the STEMMA expansion, also install R1 and R2 (1 kΩ 0805). These take flux and a fine tip but don't require hot air.
notes: R1/R2 are OPTIONAL — skip unless the STEMMA/I²C expansion ports will be used.

title: Solder surface-mount chips on C1: flash chip and MOSFETs
description: U4 is the GD25Q16C 16 Mb flash chip (SOIC-8, an 8-pin chip package). Q1 and Q2 are P-channel DMG3415U-7 MOSFETs (SOT-23, a 3-pin chip package). Use flux and drag-solder technique (run the iron tip slowly across all pins at once) for U4; Q1/Q2 are 3-pin, easy one-pin-at-a-time.
notes: Watch Q1/Q2 orientation — the DMG3415U-7 is P-channel.

title: Solder surface-mount connectors and switches on C1: µSD slot, side buttons, slide switch
description: U3 is the GCT MEM2075 microSD push/push slot (fine-pitch metal shell — solder the shell tabs first for alignment). B1 and B2 are Würth right-angle tactile buttons. SW1 is the C&K power slide switch. Install the optional J1/J2/J3 JST connectors in this step too if you're using expansion.
notes: The SD slot has both signal pins and shell tabs — tack the shell tabs first to prevent the slot from shifting while you solder the signal pins.

title: Solder the Keystone 1022 battery holder + 1022C retainer clip on C1
description: BT1 is the dual-AAA battery holder (surface-mount through-pad). These pads draw heat — use a larger tip and extra flux. Install the 1022C retainer clip, which physically retains the AAA cells.
notes: The 1022C retainer clip is easy to miss — make sure you've ordered it (Digi-Key 36-1022C-ND).

title: Solder the C2 e-paper driver module onto C1 via castellated edges (the row of half-circle pads along the board edge)
description: U2 is the pre-assembled Castellated E-Paper Driver. Drop it into its footprint on the main board and solder the castellated pads. The module generates the ±22 V and ±15 V rails the e-paper needs.
notes: You don't hand-solder the C2 internals — those were assembled at the fab house. You just solder the castellated edge pads to the C1 pads beneath.

title: Solder the 7 through-hole tactile buttons on C1 (B3–B9)
description: The menu / directional / select cluster under the screen: 7× TL1107AF180WQ. Install from the front of the board; solder on the back. Push buttons face outwards (away from the PCB).
notes: These are gullwing-style — make sure the tactile switch body seats flat against the board before soldering. Note: the BOM specifies TL1107AF180WQ (1.8 N, firmer click) but the README links to 130WQ (1.3 N, lighter touch). Either fits the same footprint.

title: Solder the Raspberry Pi Pico (U1) onto C1
description: The Pico can be installed in two ways: through-hole pin headers (if you want it removable for reflashing) or reflowed via its castellated edge pads (flush, permanent). The castellated method gives the slimmest profile.
notes: If you plan to iterate on libros firmware a lot, pin headers are a more forgiving choice. For a finished device, castellated is the right call.

title: Attach the e-paper panel via FFC (flat ribbon cable) to the C2 module
description: Slide the panel's FPC (flexible printed circuit) tail into the 24-pin 0.5 mm FFC connector (the latching clip) on the C2 module. Make sure the contact side faces the correct way (check the connector's latching direction). Close the connector latch. The GDEY042T81 and older GDEW042T2 both use the same connector — either panel works.
notes: Order a 4.2-inch B&W GoodDisplay panel — you'll almost certainly receive a GDEY042T81. Don't order the tri-color variant; libros only supports B&W.

title: Build and flash the libros firmware onto the Pico
description: Clone joeycastillo/libros from GitHub, check out the PR #11 branch, and build with the Pico SDK. The build produces a .uf2 file. To flash: hold BOOTSEL (the small button on the Pico board) while plugging it into USB — it appears as a USB drive. Drag-and-drop the .uf2 file onto it. The Pico reboots into libros.
notes: PR #11 (joeycastillo/libros#11) auto-detects which display you have via the busy-pin polarity — no configuration needed, works with both the older GDEW042T2 and the current GDEY042T81. If you happen to have an original GDEW042T2 and don't want to set up the Pico SDK, the pre-built book.uf2 at oddlyspecificobjects.com/projects/openbook/ will also work — but only with that specific display.

title: Flash babel.bin (font/glyph data) to the SPI flash chip
description: On first boot after flashing libros, the firmware needs GNU Unifont bitmap data on the external GD25Q16C flash chip (U4) to render text. Download babel.bin from the project page and flash it to the SPI flash using the libros bootloader's built-in flash utility. This takes about a minute.
notes: babel.bin is available at oddlyspecificobjects.com/projects/openbook/. It contains GNU Unifont data covering the Unicode Basic Multilingual Plane (BMP). Without it, the display will not render any text.

title: Prepare a microSD card with books
description: Format a microSD card as FAT (the standard SD card format — most cards ship this way). Copy plain UTF-8 .txt files onto it — first line of each file is the book title. Optional: use the ASCII control codes documented in the libros README for chapter breaks and formatting.
notes: Any capacity works — even 256 MB is enough for hundreds of books. For chapter breaks and formatting (bold, italic), use the ASCII control codes documented at oddlyspecificobjects.com/projects/openbook/#advanced-text-formatting. The libros-convert tool (joeycastillo/libros-convert on GitHub) can convert Standard Ebooks EPUBs into the Open Book's plain text format automatically.

title: 3D-print the case and assemble
description: Print 'Open Book Abridged - Minimal Case.stl' from the upstream repo's '3D Printed Case' directory on any FDM printer. No supports needed. Fit the completed board, insert 2× AAA batteries, and close the case. Check screw-boss dimensions in the STL for fastener sizing — the upstream does not specify a screw size.
notes: Watch your PCB thickness — the case expects a 1.0 mm board, not 1.6 mm.
